## Definition of peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM) per international consensus
Across the provided context
column2: the “international workshop”/ESC working group consensus framing of PPCM is that it is a **diagnosis of exclusion**: an **idiopathic/acquired dilated cardiomyopathy** presenting with **heart failure due to left ventricular (LV) systolic dysfunction** that develops **toward the end of pregnancy or shortly after delivery**
column3: in a patient **without an identifiable cause of heart failure** and **without recognizable heart disease prior to the last month of pregnancy**. This is explicitly stated in the ESC Heart Failure Association position statement by Sliwa et al. (2010)
column4: which also notes that the **ESC working group definition** specifies onset **in the last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum** [Reference not found in provided documents]. The same position statement further emphasizes that PPCM is a **pregnancy-associated cause of heart failure** that typically develops during the **last month of pregnancy and up to ~5 months after delivery** in women **without known cardiovascular disease**
column5: and that it is **distinct from pregnancy-related worsening of an underlying idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy (IDCM) due to volume overload** [Reference not found in provided documents].

column1: The context also includes a critique of the time-frame definitions (e.g.
column2: “last month of pregnancy” or “within 5 months postpartum”) as potentially arbitrary and leading to under-diagnosis
column3: followed by a **simplified definition** that preserves the core consensus elements: **idiopathic cardiomyopathy with heart failure secondary to LV systolic dysfunction toward the end of pregnancy or in the months after delivery
column4: with no other cause of heart failure found (diagnosis of exclusion)**; it reiterates that **LV may not be dilated** and that **LVEF is nearly always reduced below 45%** [Reference not found in provided documents]. While this “simplified definition” is presented as a refinement of the consensus concept
column5: the underlying consensus criteria remain those of **(i) timing**
column7: and **(iii) exclusion of other causes/absence of prior heart disease** (Sliwa2010a pages 3-4

column1: **Operational echocardiographic characterization** in the consensus framing includes LV systolic dysfunction typically defined by **LVEF <45%** (often “nearly always” below 45%) and/or **fractional shortening <30%** [Reference not found in provided documents]. The LV **may not be dilated**
column2: which is important for distinguishing PPCM from classic dilated cardiomyopathy phenotypes (Sliwa2010a pages 3-4

column1: ### Consolidated consensus definition (as supported by the provided context)
Based on the ESC working group/international workshop consensus described in the Sliwa position statement

> **PPCM is an idiopathic/acquired cardiomyopathy presenting with heart failure due to LV systolic dysfunction that develops toward the end of pregnancy or within a defined postpartum window (last month of pregnancy or within ~5 months after delivery)
column2: in a patient with no identifiable cause of heart failure and no recognizable pre-existing heart disease prior to the last month of pregnancy; LV systolic dysfunction is typically LVEF <45% (and/or fractional shortening <30%)
column3: and LV dilation may be absent.** (Sliwa2010a pages 3-4

column1: ## Differentiating de novo PPCM from chronic HFrEF predating pregnancy
### 1) The consensus differentiation is fundamentally based on “diagnosis of exclusion” and prior disease status
The consensus definition explicitly requires **no recognizable heart disease prior to the last month of pregnancy** [Reference not found in provided documents]. Therefore
column2: the conceptual boundary between **de novo PPCM** and **underlying chronic HFrEF that predates pregnancy** is whether the patient’s LV systolic dysfunction represents a **new onset** condition temporally and etiologically linked to pregnancy

column1: The Sliwa position statement highlights that PPCM is **not simply pregnancy-related worsening of an underlying idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy due to volume overload**
column2: reinforcing that “de novo” PPCM should not be reclassified as PPCM when the underlying cardiomyopathy existed before pregnancy [Reference not found in provided documents].

column1: ### 2) Clinical timing and phenotype patterns support differentiation
The context notes that PPCM **often presents after delivery**
column2: with a variable course that can include rapid progression to end-stage heart failure or full recovery [Reference not found in provided documents]. In contrast
column3: conditions such as **idiopathic/familial dilated cardiomyopathy** that are unmasked by pregnancy-related hemodynamic stress typically present **earlier in pregnancy (often by the 2nd trimester)** and often have **larger cardiac dimensions** [Reference not found in provided documents]. This timing/phenotype contrast is used to guide differentiation in practice
column4: but the consensus framework still requires exclusion of pre-existing disease [Reference not found in provided documents].

column1: ### 3) Recommended evaluation includes history and testing to establish “new onset” status
The Sliwa position statement describes that PPCM is a **diagnosis of exclusion** requiring thorough investigation for alternative etiologies
column2: including both cardiac and non-cardiac causes [Reference not found in provided documents]. It also specifies diagnostic tools used to establish diagnosis and prognosis (ECG
column6: including MRI features that can help differentiate myocarditis (late gadolinium enhancement) and imaging to rule out LV thrombus [Reference not found in provided documents].

column3: investigators obtained and confirmed **history of preexisting cardiac symptoms/signs** to distinguish **new-onset PPCM** from prior cardiac disease (Sliwa2010 pages 1-2). While this is from a bromocriptine pilot study rather than a formal consensus definition
column4: it operationalizes the consensus requirement that PPCM must not have recognizable prior heart disease (Sliwa2010 pages 1-2

column1: ### 4) Genetic/familial cardiomyopathy can complicate classification
The context notes that some familial cases may represent **initial manifestation of familial dilated cardiomyopathy rather than true PPCM**
column2: raising diagnostic uncertainty when family history suggests inherited disease [Reference not found in provided documents]. This underscores that “de novo PPCM” requires careful assessment for evidence of pre-existing cardiomyopathy
column3: including family screening when appropriate (Sliwa2010a pages 5-6

The provided excerpts do **not** include a verbatim “most recent international consensus” definition beyond the ESC working group/international workshop time window and exclusion criteria described above
column2: nor do they provide a fully explicit
column3: consensus-based algorithmic rule for distinguishing de novo PPCM from chronic HFrEF predating pregnancy beyond the requirement of **no prior recognizable heart disease** and the use of clinical history plus diagnostic evaluation (Sliwa2010 pages 1-1

column1: ### Bottom line
- **Consensus definition:** PPCM is a **diagnosis of exclusion**—new-onset **LV systolic dysfunction with heart failure** occurring **toward the end of pregnancy or within ~5 months postpartum**
column3: in a patient with **no identifiable cause** and **no recognizable pre-existing heart disease prior to the last month of pregnancy** (Sliwa2010a pages 3-4

column1: - **De novo vs pre-existing HFrEF:** “De novo PPCM” requires evidence that the cardiomyopathy is **not pre-existing** (no prior recognizable heart disease/symptoms)
column2: whereas underlying chronic HFrEF that predates pregnancy should not meet PPCM criteria; differentiation relies on **history** and **diagnostic exclusion**
column3: supported by timing/phenotype patterns (PPCM often postpartum; unmasked chronic DCM often earlier in pregnancy with larger dimensions) (Sliwa2010a pages 3-4
